source.title: Putting fear in its place: remapping of hippocampal place cells during fear conditioning

confidence_rationale: Foundational demonstration that aversive reinforcement induces place-cell remapping even when the physical environment is unchanged, and that the remapping is contingent on the rat's *learned fear of the environment* (contextual > auditory fear). This is the canonical evidence for fear/threat as a privileged map-tagging affect stream.
failure_signatures: Place-cell remapping is not driven only by sensory environment changes — affective context (learned fear of an environment) is sufficient to drive remapping.
Auditory fear conditioning produces less remapping than contextual fear conditioning, so the remapping is fear-of-environment-specific, not generic-fear-modulation.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Fear conditioning causes hippocampal place cells to remap their preferred firing locations in the conditioned environment, with significantly more remapping for contextual than auditory fear conditioning.
mapping.ree_translation: Fear/threat is a second affect stream (alongside reward) for which the hippocampal map demonstrably reorganizes — at the level of place-field location, not just firing rate. This is the canonical existence proof for the SD-011-generalization that multiple affect channels have privileged map-tagging.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper does not dissect whether the remapping reflects sensory-discriminative fear (predator detection, shock locus) versus affective-motivational fear (general dread). For SD-011's specific dual-nociceptive split (sensory vs affective), the fear-conditioning literature is on the affective side — direct shock receptor signals are more likely to be in primary somatosensory streams.
mapping.source_context: Sprague-Dawley rats, dorsal CA1 single-unit recording, contextual vs auditory fear conditioning.

confidence_components.notes: JNeurosci 2004, LeDoux/Blair labs — canonical work, widely replicated. Mapping fidelity is high for the existence question and moderate for the SD-011 sensory/affective split because the paradigm doesn't isolate the two.
